questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.section: 1.1 Revision on Natural Numbers and Integers
questions.question: Given that \(a = 38\) and \(b = 4\), the unique integers \(q\) and \(r\) satisfying \(a = bq + r\) with \(0 \leq r < b\) are:
questions.hint: Divide 38 by 4. The remainder must be less than 4.
questions.explanation: 38 ÷ 4 = 9 remainder 2, so \(38 = 4 \times 9 + 2\). Option B remainder 6 ≥4, C remainder negative, D remainder 4 not less than divisor.

source_trace.section_anchor: 1.1 Revision on Natural Numbers and Integers

explanations_tiered.definition: Euclid's division lemma expresses division as \(a = bq + r\) with unique integers q (quotient) and r (remainder) satisfying \(0 \leq r < b\).
explanations_tiered.concept_summary: For 38 divided by 4, q = floor(38/4) = 9, r = 38 - 4×9 = 2, satisfying 0≤2<4.
explanations_tiered.beginner: 38 apples for 4 people: each gets 9 apples, 2 left. So q=9, r=2.
explanations_tiered.intermediate: 38 ÷ 4 = 9 remainder 2, so 38 = 4×9 + 2. Remainder 2 is less than divisor 4.
explanations_tiered.advanced: By division algorithm: q = ⌊38/4⌋ = 9, r = 38 - 4×9 = 2. Uniqueness follows from 0 ≤ r < b.
reasoning_steps: Find integer q such that bq ≤ a < b(q+1).
misconceptions: Using q = 8, r = 6 violates r < 4 because 6 can be further divided by 4.
Allowing negative remainder (r = -2) which fails the non-negative condition.
explanations_tiered.revision_note: 38 = 4×9 + 2, q=9, r=2. Remainder must be < divisor.

glossary_entry.definition: Integer result of division in Euclid's lemma.

flashcard.front: Find q and r for 38 = 4q + r with 0≤r<4.

publishing_metadata.workbook_section: 1.1 Revision on Natural Numbers and Integers
publishing_metadata.solution_manual_note: Answer A. q=9, r=2. Option B r=6 invalid, C r negative, D r=4 not <4.

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.section: 1.1 Revision on Natural Numbers and Integers
questions.question: Which of the following statements correctly defines a composite number?

options.A: A natural number with exactly two factors
options.B: A natural number with more than two factors
options.C: A natural number with exactly one factor
options.D: A natural number with no factors

questions.hint: Composite numbers have additional factors beyond 1 and itself.
questions.explanation: Composite numbers are natural numbers greater than 1 that have more than two factors. Option A defines prime numbers; C describes 1 (neither prime nor composite); D is false (every number has at least 1 and itself).

source_trace.section_anchor: 1.1 Revision on Natural Numbers and Integers
source_trace.content_anchor: composite number has more than two factors

explanations_tiered.definition: A composite number is a natural number greater than 1 that has at least one divisor other than 1 and itself.
explanations_tiered.concept_summary: Composite numbers have more than two factors, unlike primes which have exactly two factors.
explanations_tiered.beginner: Composite numbers like 4,6,8,9 have factors besides 1 and itself. 4's factors: 1,2,4 (three factors).
explanations_tiered.intermediate: Definition: composite = natural number >1 with more than two factors. Option B matches.
explanations_tiered.advanced: Formally: n > 1 is composite iff ∃ integers a,b such that 1 < a ≤ b < n and n = a × b.
reasoning_steps: Recall definition of composite number from PDF.
Count number of factors: primes have 2, composites have >2.
Select option with 'more than two factors'.
misconceptions: Confusing composite with prime (exactly two factors).
Thinking 1 is composite (1 has only one factor).
explanations_tiered.revision_note: Composite: more than two factors. 4,6,8,9 are composite.

glossary_entry.definition: Natural number >1 with more than two positive divisors.

flashcard.back: A natural number with more than two factors.

publishing_metadata.flashcard_back: Natural number with more than two factors.
publishing_metadata.glossary_definition: Number with more than two factors.
publishing_metadata.teacher_note: Emphasize 1 is neither prime nor composite.
publishing_metadata.workbook_section: 1.1 Revision on Natural Numbers and Integers
publishing_metadata.solution_manual_note: Answer B. Option A defines prime, C describes 1, D false.

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.section: 1.1 Revision on Natural Numbers and Integers
questions.question: A number is divisible by 6 if it is divisible by:
questions.explanation: A number is divisible by 6 if and only if it is divisible by both 2 and 3, because 6 = 2 × 3 and gcd(2,3)=1. Options A, B are insufficient; C includes 4 which is not required.

source_trace.section_anchor: 1.1 Revision on Natural Numbers and Integers
source_trace.structural_element: A number is divisible by 6, if it is divisible by 2 and 3
source_trace.content_anchor: divisible by 2 and 3 implies divisible by 6

explanations_tiered.definition: A number is divisible by 6 if it is divisible by both 2 and 3, since 6 = 2 × 3 and 2 and 3 are coprime.
explanations_tiered.concept_summary: Divisibility by 6 requires the number to be even (divisible by 2) and the sum of its digits divisible by 3.
explanations_tiered.beginner: If a number is even AND the sum of its digits is a multiple of 3, then it's divisible by 6.
explanations_tiered.intermediate: 6 = 2 × 3, and gcd(2,3)=1, so divisibility by 6 ⇔ divisibility by 2 AND 3.
explanations_tiered.advanced: Since 2 and 3 are coprime, LCM(2,3)=6, and divisibility by LCM requires divisibility by both factors.
reasoning_steps: A number divisible by 6 must be divisible by both 2 and 3.
misconceptions: Thinking divisibility by 4 and 2 works (4 not needed).
Believing just being even is enough (must also pass 3 test).

glossary_entry.example_equation: 90 is divisible by 6 (even and sum 9 divisible by 3)

flashcard.front: What conditions make a number divisible by 6?

publishing_metadata.flashcard_front: Divisible by 6 requires divisibility by which numbers?
publishing_metadata.glossary_definition: Even and digit sum multiple of 3.
publishing_metadata.workbook_section: 1.1 Revision on Natural Numbers and Integers
publishing_metadata.solution_manual_note: Answer D. Options A and B insufficient, C includes unnecessary 4.

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.section: 1.1 Revision on Natural Numbers and Integers
questions.question: What is the prime factorization of 168?
questions.hint: Divide 168 repeatedly by prime numbers (2,3,5,7...) until 1.
questions.explanation: 168 ÷ 2 = 84, ÷2 = 42, ÷2 = 21, ÷3 = 7, ÷7 = 1. So 168 = 2³ × 3 × 7. Option A has 2², C includes extra 3², D has both errors.

source_trace.section_anchor: 1.1 Revision on Natural Numbers and Integers

explanations_tiered.definition: Prime factorization expresses a composite number as a product of prime numbers.
explanations_tiered.concept_summary: Divide by smallest primes repeatedly until quotient becomes prime.
reasoning_steps: Then divide by 3, then 5, then 7 as needed.
Collect all prime factors with exponents.
misconceptions: Forgetting to divide by 2 three times (only doing twice gives 2²).
Including extra factor of 3 (21 ÷ 3 = 7, so only one 3).

glossary_entry.definition: Product of prime numbers that equals the number.

publishing_metadata.learning_objective_tag: Express composite numbers in prime factorization form
publishing_metadata.workbook_section: 1.1 Revision on Natural Numbers and Integers
publishing_metadata.solution_manual_note: Answer B. 168 = 2³ × 3 × 7. Option A missing one 2, C extra 3, D both errors.

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.section: 1.1 Revision on Natural Numbers and Integers
questions.subsection: 1.1.5 Greatest common factor and least common multiple
questions.question: Given \(\text{GCF}(15,42) = 3\), what is \(\text{LCM}(15,42)\) using the relationship \(\text{GCF}(a,b) \times \text{LCM}(a,b) = a \times b\)?
questions.explanation: Using \(\text{GCF}(a,b) \times \text{LCM}(a,b) = a \times b\), we have \(3 \times \text{LCM} = 15 \times 42 = 630\). Thus \(\text{LCM} = 630 ÷ 3 = 210\). Options A, C, D do not satisfy the equation.

source_trace.section_anchor: 1.1 Revision on Natural Numbers and Integers
source_trace.subsection_anchor: 1.1.5 Greatest common factor and least common multiple

explanations_tiered.definition: For any two natural numbers, the product of their GCF and LCM equals the product of the numbers themselves.
explanations_tiered.concept_summary: This relationship allows finding LCM when GCF is known, or vice versa.
explanations_tiered.beginner: Multiply 15 and 42 = 630. Divide by GCF (3) gives LCM = 210.
explanations_tiered.advanced: Since GCF(a,b) × LCM(a,b) = |ab|, LCM = |ab| / GCF(a,b). For positive integers, LCM = ab / GCF.
reasoning_steps: Recall formula: GCF × LCM = a × b.
misconceptions: Multiplying a and b but forgetting to divide by GCF.
Dividing by wrong number (using LCM instead).

glossary_entry.definition: Product of GCF and LCM equals product of the two numbers.

publishing_metadata.glossary_definition: Product of GCF and LCM = product of numbers.
publishing_metadata.teacher_note: Use Venn diagram to illustrate GCF and LCM overlap.
publishing_metadata.learning_objective_tag: Use relationship between GCF and LCM
publishing_metadata.workbook_section: 1.1 Revision on Natural Numbers and Integers

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.subsection: 1.2.1 Representation of rational numbers by decimals
questions.question: Convert the decimal 3.7 into a fraction.
questions.hint: 3.7 has one digit after the decimal, so denominator is 10.
questions.explanation: 3.7 = \(\frac{37}{10}\) because 3.7 × 10 = 37. Option B is 0.37, C is 0.037, D is approximately 0.4286.

source_trace.subsection_anchor: 1.2.1 Representation of rational numbers by decimals

explanations_tiered.definition: A terminating decimal can be written as a fraction with denominator a power of 10.
explanations_tiered.concept_summary: Count digits after decimal: 1 digit → denominator 10; 2 digits → denominator 100, etc.
explanations_tiered.intermediate: 3.7 = 37/10 because moving decimal one place right multiplies by 10: 3.7 × 10 = 37, so divide back: 37/10.
worked_solution: Step 1: Identify decimal places: 1 digit after decimal.
misconceptions: Using denominator 100 (two digits) for one digit decimal.
Writing as 3/7 (confusing decimal with fraction).

glossary_entry.definition: Write over power of 10 based on decimal places.

publishing_metadata.teacher_note: Use place value chart: tenths place.

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.subsection: 1.2.2 Conversion of repeating decimals into fractions
questions.structural_anchor: Conversion of repeating decimals into fractions
questions.question: Convert the repeating decimal \(0.\overline{12}\) into a fraction in simplest form.
questions.hint: Let x = 0.121212..., multiply by 100, subtract, solve.
questions.explanation: Let x = 0.121212... Then 100x = 12.121212... Subtract: 99x = 12, so x = 12/99 = 4/33. Option A is unsimplified; B is 0.12 (terminating); D is 1.333...

source_trace.subsection_anchor: 1.2.2 Conversion of repeating decimals into fractions

explanations_tiered.definition: A repeating decimal with period 2 digits can be converted by multiplying by 100 and subtracting.
explanations_tiered.concept_summary: For two-digit repeating block, denominator becomes 99, then simplify.
reasoning_steps: Subtract original to eliminate repeating part.
misconceptions: Using denominator 100 (terminating decimal rule) instead of 99.
Not simplifying 12/99 to 4/33.

publishing_metadata.solution_manual_note: Answer C. 0.12̅ = 12/99 = 4/33. Option A unsimplified, B=0.12, D=1.333...

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.question: Which of the following is an irrational number between 3 and 4?
questions.hint: Find which square root lies between 3²=9 and 4²=16 and is not a perfect square.
questions.explanation: √10 ≈ 3.162, between 3 and 4, and 10 is not a perfect square so it is irrational. √9=3 (rational), √16=4 (rational), 22/7≈3.142857 (repeating decimal).

explanations_tiered.definition: An irrational number cannot be expressed as a fraction of two integers; its decimal is non-terminating and non-repeating.
explanations_tiered.concept_summary: For a square root to be irrational, the radicand must not be a perfect square. √10 is irrational and lies between 3 and 4.
explanations_tiered.beginner: 3²=9, 4²=16. √10 is about 3.16, between 3 and 4, and 10 is not a perfect square, so it's irrational.
explanations_tiered.intermediate: Since 9 < 10 < 16, 3 < √10 < 4. 10 is not a perfect square, so √10 is irrational.
explanations_tiered.advanced: √10 is irrational by proof by contradiction (if √10 = p/q in lowest terms, then 10q² = p², contradiction to unique prime factorization).
reasoning_steps: All are irrational except perfect squares (none here).
misconceptions: Thinking 22/7 is irrational (it's rational, repeating decimal).
Believing √10 is rational because 10 is even.

glossary_entry.definition: Square root of a non-perfect square is irrational.

flashcard.front: Name an irrational number between 3 and 4.

visual_system.visual_description: Number line from 3 to 4 with tick marks at integers 3 and 4. √10 marked at approximately 3.162, with label.
visual_system.caption: Figure: √10 lies between 3 and 4.
visual_system.accessibility_alt_text: Number line showing √10 at position 3.162 between 3 and 4.

publishing_metadata.flashcard_front: Find an irrational number between 3 and 4.
publishing_metadata.learning_objective_tag: Identify irrational numbers between given integers
publishing_metadata.solution_manual_note: Answer B. √9=3, √16=4, 22/7 rational, √10 irrational between 3 and 4.

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.question: Simplify \(\frac{\sqrt{18}}{\sqrt{2}}\).
questions.explanation: \(\frac{\sqrt{18}}{\sqrt{2}} = \sqrt{\frac{18}{2}} = \sqrt{9} = 3\). Option B is 9, C is √9 which simplifies to 3 but not simplified form, D is 4.

explanations_tiered.definition: Division of square roots: √a / √b = √(a/b) for a,b > 0.
explanations_tiered.concept_summary: Combine radicands under a single radical, then simplify.
misconceptions: Subtracting radicands: √18/√2 ≠ √16 = 4.
Not simplifying √9 to 3 (leaving as √9).

publishing_metadata.solution_manual_note: Answer A. √18/√2 = √(18/2)=√9=3. Option B=9, C=√9 (unsimplified), D=4.

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.question: Solve for \(x\): \(|x| = 8\).
questions.hint: Absolute value represents distance from zero.
questions.explanation: |x| = 8 means x is 8 units from zero on the number line, so x = 8 or x = -8. Option A and B give only one solution; D is false because solutions exist.

explanations_tiered.definition: Absolute value |x| is the distance from x to 0 on the number line; always non-negative.
explanations_tiered.concept_summary: |x| = a (a>0) has two solutions: x = a and x = -a.
explanations_tiered.beginner: |x|=8 means x is 8 away from zero. So x could be 8 or -8.
explanations_tiered.intermediate: By definition, if |x| = 8, then x = 8 or x = -8 because both satisfy |8|=8 and |-8|=8.
explanations_tiered.advanced: |x| = 8 → x = ±8. This follows from the piecewise definition: if x≥0, x=8; if x<0, -x=8 → x=-8.
worked_solution: Step 2: Distance 8 means x = 8 or x = -8.
reasoning_steps: Identify that absolute value yields non-negative output.
Consider both positive and negative possibilities.
misconceptions: Only giving positive solution (forgetting negative).
Thinking absolute value makes all numbers positive, so only positive.

glossary_entry.definition: Equation of form |x| = a has solutions x = a or x = -a (a>0).

visual_system.visual_description: Number line from -10 to 10. Points at -8 and 8 are marked with filled circles, both labeled. Arrows indicate distance 8 from zero to each.
visual_system.caption: Figure: Two points at distance 8 from origin.
visual_system.accessibility_alt_text: Number line with points at -8 and 8, both 8 units from zero.

publishing_metadata.teacher_note: Use number line to show both solutions symmetrically.
publishing_metadata.solution_manual_note: Answer C. |x|=8 → x=8 or x=-8. Options A, B single solutions; D no solution false.
